retrieved_skills.skill_text: The architecture, including the shape and style of the houses and use of red brick, is common in rural or small-town Brazil.
The architecture is simple, with painted white homes and exposed brick, typical of rural towns in Brazil.
The red tile roofs and the style of the doors and windows fit with towns in Boyacá or Cundinamarca departments in Colombia.
The road and street design are found in historic centers of Brazilian cities.
The absence of English, Cyrillic, or Asian characters and street signage suggests a Spanish-speaking country.
Overall, the combination of landscape, climate, and road quality closely matches what is found in rural parts of the Caribbean, specifically in Cuba.

The overall construction style, the absence of visible commercial signage, and lack of Spanish language in the image suggest an Ecuadorian city rather than a Peruvian or Colombian one, which often have more decorative urban elements.
The vegetation is too dense for most Latin American locations, and the style of the concrete road edges matches those in Southeast Asia.
The steep street and hilly terrain suggest an Andean town, which matches the geography of central Colombia.
The presence of lush, tropical vegetation including palms and dense undergrowth matches what is found in subtropical climates, particularly in South and Southeast Asia.
The presence of dense coconut palms and tropical vegetation suggests a humid tropical climate, typical of coastal South Asia.
The lack of any visible Spanish text or signage makes Brazil more likely than neighboring countries.
